policy_statement: Reform power subsidies and transport-related subsidies to offset perverse incentives for wasteful consumption (NEEAP initiative).
mechanism_description: Reforms power and transport-related subsidies to remove incentives for wasteful consumption, reducing energy and fuel use and associated greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Price signal reform → ↓ energy/fuel demand → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Government reduces or removes electricity and transport-fuel subsidies (and/or restructures them to avoid encouraging overconsumption), altering end-user prices and consumption behavior. (2) Mediating process: Higher effective energy prices and improved marginal cost signals drive reduced demand, efficiency investments, fuel switching, and reduced vehicle-km/energy services where elastic. (3) Outcome: Lower combustion of fossil fuels in power generation and transport leads to reduced CO₂ (and some co-pollutant) emissions relative to the subsidized baseline.
dominant_pathway_test: Removing energy/fuel subsidies most strongly reduces GHG emissions by cutting fossil energy consumption via higher effective prices and less wasteful use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: subsidy/price reform primarily affects energy and fuel demand, which directly reduces fossil fuel combustion and GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while subsidy reform can also improve fiscal efficiency or equity, the mechanism described and the climate-relevant effect is emissions reduction from lower energy use, which is classic Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency (which would center on delivering the same service with less input) or Adaptation/NBS.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a reform shifts subsidies from fossil fuels to renewables/efficiency, mitigation still dominates (emissions fall via cleaner supply and/or reduced demand). If reforms unintentionally increase emissions (e.g., switching from subsidized electricity to diesel generators), that would be an implementation-specific exception, not the canonical mechanism here.  
4) Applies to all instances: any policy that removes/restructures power and transport fossil-energy subsidies to curb wasteful consumption shares the same dominant mitigation pathway of reduced fossil energy use and emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: whether the main lever is electricity, transport fuels, demand response, efficiency uptake, or fuel switching should be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include rebound/behavioral responses, compensatory measures that blunt price signals, or substitution to higher-emitting off-grid fuels; however, under the stated mechanism (removing incentives for wasteful consumption), the dominant expected effect remains emissions mitigation.
key_indicators: “Subsidy reform”; “reforms to both power subsidies and those that affect transport”; “offset perverse incentives”; “wasteful consumption”
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollution (SO₂/NOₓ/PM) from lower fuel combustion; improved fiscal balance/efficiency of public spending; reduced congestion and fuel import dependence
instance_edge_case_notes: Although “subsidy reform” is not explicitly framed as a tax, it operates as a price-signal instrument by removing price suppression; classified as pricing-mechanism rather than incentive-program because it primarily withdraws an existing subsidy rather than offering new targeted support.
